result:
Sometimes I sit quietly and wonder what it would feel like to be the color red—nothing subtle, no pink blush or brick mosque dome, just the shameless, locomotive red that reheats every sunset every single day. If consciousness were a dye, I imagine it would behave like tempera painted on skin: it cracks when you laugh, pools in sun wrinkles, flakes off when you cross your arms too hard and scratch yourself accidentally. Being a loud color must be a dare in a society that has invented rules for ambulances to be red but keeps them silent when they take away people nobody writes songs about.  

I wonder whether poppies growing beside the railway remember the spilled blood they were named after. A flower must carry a strange etiquette: standing perfectly still after a train screams past so as not to offend the apostles of punctuality, but also leaning downward afterward as if to whisper, “We saw everything.” I picture them pressing small, red affidavits into the cinders and hoping the soil can read.  

The night sky confuses me most because it is an argument against calendars. Stars don’t date anything; they simply flare when no one is looking, then wait until some forgotten mammal invents a telescope so that light billions of years old can finally close its own eyes and die. Maybe that’s why moths keep flying into porch bulbs: existential privacy. They want to slam the door of another timeline before the electric bill does it first.  

I read once that forests can grow inside abandoned pianos: spores germinate on the tangled felt, strings oxidize into roots, the lid becomes a lost horizon. Cities should try this—replace streetlamps with ambitious uprights, allow thunder to rehearse octaves for once. Children would learn night music before learning fear; the alphabet would be replaced by chords asking who left the animals so thoroughly alone.  

When it rains hard, I hear the world apologize for all the skin it never got to touch. Each drop is a syllable that forgot its vowel; together they spell “I’m sorry you thought touching had to be gentle.” My umbrella, cheap plastic printed with cartoon ghosts, keeps trying to open itself inside-out because even manufactured apparitions know you can either be shelter or witness, never both. The ghosts let the water through on purpose—they want to feel what evaporated from all the people already gone.  

Here’s a secret: every time I pass the bakery at 5:30 a.m., I wave to the ceiling fans. They rotate hostage smells of warm dough like petitioners who know their motion is temporary but their guilt is inherited. One morning the shortest fan stuttered and stopped mid-salute. The baker cursed, tightened a screw, it whirred again. I’m undecided as to whether we just saved a life or undid a suicide.  

Lately I borrow hours from tomorrow to sit on nobody’s porch and open a brand-new box of silence. Its manual claims it is compatible with guilt, snow, and certain wavelengths of parrot. Use during eclipses to avoid epiphanies. I haven’t figured out where to place the batteries, so I simply keep the box wrapped in yesterday’s newspaper. Headlines about abandoned mines still try to crawl out of the comics page; they think jokes are softer geology.  

Between some lines I have left blank on purpose, as refuges for any future earthquake that takes handwriting personally. You can press your own tremor here, lightly, like a signature on a mirror fogged with travel plans.  

Will you?

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
